lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: “Caesar, beware of Brutus; take heed of Cassius; come not near Casca;

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: have an eye to Cinna; trust not Trebonius; mark well Metellus Cimber:

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Decius Brutus loves thee not: thou hast wronged Caius Ligarius. There is

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: but one mind in all these men, and it is bent against

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Caesar. If thou beest not immortal, look about you: security gives way

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: to conspiracy. The mighty gods defend thee! Thy lover, “Artemidorus.”

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Here will I stand till Caesar pass along,

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: And as a suitor will I give him this.

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: My heart laments that virtue cannot live

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Out of the teeth of emulation.

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: If thou read this, O Caesar, thou mayst live;

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: If not, the Fates with traitors do contrive.

glosses.definition: opening of Artemidorus's petition — naming each conspirator in turn; Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* records this warning
complacency opens the way to plots — the tragic moral of Caesar's refusal to read this letter
in Elizabethan usage, a devoted friend or well-wisher; NOT romantic
petitioner; one who presents a request
the biting jaws of envy; ambitious rivalry figured as a predator
the three Fates (classical goddesses who spin, measure, and cut the thread of life) conspire with the traitors

annotations.title: Artemidorus of Cnidos and the warning that was not read
annotations.body:
The historical Artemidorus was a Greek teacher of rhetoric in Rome, according to Plutarch a friend of Brutus's circle who had learned of the plot and tried to warn Caesar. Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* §65 reports that Artemidorus pressed a written petition on Caesar at the Forum, saying 'Caesar, read this alone, and quickly'; Caesar took the paper but was prevented from reading it by the press of other petitioners.

Shakespeare preserves the historical detail and gives it a brief solo scene, heightening its pathos. The letter's structure — name each conspirator, predict the plot's direction — is the exact form of information Caesar needs. In 3.1 Caesar will encounter Artemidorus in person and tell him (at L7) 'What touches us ourself shall be last served.' That refusal to prioritise personal over public business is the moment the warning is lost. Shakespeare uses the historical incident to make a specific point: Caesar dies partly because he has internalised a public-service ideology that puts his own safety behind the queue.

trials.prompt: Who is Artemidorus, and what does he know?
options: A priest who has read entrails
A teacher of rhetoric with ties to Brutus's circle, who has learned of the conspiracy and attempts to warn Caesar by written petition — the incident is in Plutarch

trials.prompt: What does 'security gives way to conspiracy' mean in Artemidorus's letter?
options: Complacency about one's safety opens the way for plots — the tragic moral of Caesar's refusal to read this letter in 3.1
Artemidorus is offering Caesar a bodyguard
A criticism of the Roman Senate's lax security

trials.prompt: Artemidorus signs his letter 'Thy lover, Artemidorus' — what does 'lover' mean here?
options: It is romantic in the modern sense
'Lover' in Elizabethan usage = devoted friend, well-wisher; the same meaning Brutus uses in 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers' at 3.2
